Helping partners integrate Expedia's APIs

Expedia was only offering connectivity providers access to roughly 40% of its capabilities — far behind competitors. The solution: a technical document that was as much a marketing pitch as an implementation guide, written to persuade and to enable in equal measure.

87.7%
API adoption success rate after the document launched
100%
Of providers said the doc was integral to successful implementation
→All
Providers wanted similar documentation for all future API integrations

Overview

A connectivity gap that was costing Expedia partners

Hotel partners don't list exclusively on Expedia. They use connectivity software — independent platforms that distribute rate and inventory changes across every channel simultaneously. When a hotel updates its rates for a holiday weekend, that one change should propagate to Expedia, Booking.com, Airbnb, and every other channel automatically.

But for that to work, each travel website has to give connectivity providers access to its APIs. And Expedia was only offering access to roughly 40% of its capabilities — compared to competitors who were providing full access. The result: partners had to make changes twice, Expedia was showing incorrect rates and outdated content, and some partners had stopped listing on Expedia altogether.

The team decided the best way to close the gap was to make it as easy as possible for connectivity providers to build the missing integrations. That meant creating a technical document that could serve two audiences at once: the decision-makers who needed to be persuaded it was worth building, and the engineers and product managers who needed to know exactly how to build it.

The multi-channel problem — partners managing separate listings across Booking.com, Priceline, Airbnb and more
How it should work — one update through connectivity software propagating to all channels simultaneously
Technical writing Content strategy User research Competitive analysis UX best practices IA

The problem

Two separate failures, one document to fix both

The challenge wasn't just technical — it was motivational. Connectivity providers had to choose to prioritise building Expedia's integrations over everything else on their roadmap. And once they decided to build, they needed clear enough guidance to actually succeed. The document had to solve both problems simultaneously.

The "why bother" problem

Expedia was at ~40% capability coverage — far behind Booking.com and other competitors offering full access
Providers had full roadmaps — building new integrations was a real cost that needed a compelling business case
Hotel partners were churning — some had stopped listing on Expedia entirely because of the rate and content mismatch
No persuasive case existed — there was nothing that clearly articulated why implementing Expedia's functionalities would help providers acquire and retain hotel partners

The "how to build it" problem

Audience was mixed — connectivity providers ranged from large platforms with dedicated UX teams to smaller shops with engineers doing everything
Expedia's flows already existed — but hadn't been documented in a way external teams could follow
No shared vocabulary — terms like "unit spaces," "cancellation policy," and "other fees" needed defining before the technical specs could land
Error states and edge cases — without guidance, providers would build inconsistent implementations that created a worse partner experience
The broken state — the connectivity gap meant hotels had to duplicate changes just for Expedia, or risk incorrect listings
The partner's dilemma — manual updates per channel vs. a single connectivity update that should reach everywhere

Understanding the audience

Three readers, one document

Discovery research focused on understanding who would actually read this document — their roles, what they look for in API integrations, and what would make their lives easier. The document needed to work across three distinct reader types without feeling like it was written for none of them.

Decision-makers

Product leads and business owners evaluating whether the integration was worth the engineering investment. They needed the business case upfront: what's in it for us, and what's in it for our hotel partners?

Engineers

The builders. They needed precise technical specs, clear requirements for each API component, and enough context to understand why each part was designed the way it was — not just what to build.

Product managers

Coordinators between business and engineering. They needed the structural overview, the flow diagrams, and the UX guidance — especially providers who didn't have a dedicated design resource to lean on.

User personas from discovery research — Business Admin (product director) and Developer at connectivity software providers, each with distinct needs

Research

Looking at what competitors were already doing

Before writing a word, I conducted a competitive analysis of how Booking.com, Agoda, Priceline, and several non-travel companies were publishing similar documents. The goal: understand what made technical documentation actually useful — what resonated, what fell flat, what felt informative, and what was superfluous.

Key finding: the best documents led with the value proposition before the specs. They assumed the reader needed to be sold before they needed to be instructed. That became the structural anchor for Expedia's document.

Writing principle: Tell them what this is, why hotel partners like it, and why it's worth everyone's time — before you ask them to read a single line of technical specification.

What we found in the competitive analysis

What worked

Leading with business value before technical detail. Plain-language overviews before specifications. Visual flow examples for each component. Explicit UX guidance for teams without design resources.

What fell flat

Spec-first structures that assumed buy-in the reader hadn't given yet. Documents that assumed a dedicated UX team. Jargon-heavy intros that required expertise to parse before reaching the value proposition.

Competitive analysis — reviewing documentation from Booking.com, Agoda, Priceline and non-travel companies to identify what worked and what fell flat

Approach

A document structure that persuades before it instructs

The document had two explicit goals: the marketing pitch (why should you build this?) and the implementation guide (how do you build this?). I structured it so each section served both — the business case was woven into the technical overview, and the specs were grounded in proven examples from Expedia's own system.

Opening pitch

Decision-makers first

Led with the "why" — why implementing Expedia's functionalities helps connectivity providers acquire and retain hotel partners. Direct and persuasive, not buried in context. Written for the person who has to approve the roadmap item.

Component overview

Plain language before specs

A high-level explanation of each component — property, unit/unit spaces, cancellation policy, other fees — and how each is intended to function. Written so readers understood the shape of the system before diving into technical requirements.

Flow examples

Show, don't just tell

Since we didn't know if providers had a dedicated UX team, I provided examples of how each flow operates using Expedia's own property management system as the reference point. This gave providers a proven experience to model — not a blank canvas to interpret.

Technical requirements

Exact specs per component

For each capability, I spelled out exactly what needed to get built — no ambiguity, no assumptions about prior knowledge. Requirements were scoped per component so engineers could work through the document sequentially or jump to what they needed.

UX guidance

For teams without design resources

Knowing many providers lacked dedicated UX resourcing, I included explicit guidance on UX best practices: error states, accessibility requirements, CTA conventions, and designing for simplicity. This section alone addressed one of the biggest gaps the competitive analysis had identified.

Opening pitch in the document — direct benefits-first framing with data: 97% of net book value comes from properties using the rates, availability, and inventory API
Component overview — how to organise a property and its units, with a relationship diagram showing Property → Unit/Unit space → Rate plan → Cancellation policy and Fee set
Plain-language section explanations — unit/unit space defined clearly before any technical specs, with edge cases (New York City per-unit-space taxes) called out explicitly
Flow examples and use cases — Expedia's own flows used as the reference implementation, with step-by-step use cases spelling out exactly what needs to get built
Supplementary materials — UX guidelines for teams without design resources (error messages, accessibility, CTAs) and a product management glossary defining terms like CTA, radio button, and side panel

Process

Discovery to delivery

01
User research
Got familiar with the three reader types — decision-makers, engineers, product managers — and what each needed to get value from the document. Mapped their goals and pain points before writing a word.
02
Competitive analysis
Reviewed API documentation from Booking.com, Agoda, Priceline, and non-travel companies. Identified structural and tonal patterns that worked, and anti-patterns that created friction.
03
Document architecture
Designed the two-goal structure: marketing pitch layered into technical overview, with Expedia's own flows as the reference implementation. Built for multi-audience reading — linear or non-linear.
04
Writing & validation
Wrote with an emphasis on being direct and persuasive throughout — not just in the intro. Validated with connectivity providers and iterated on clarity, completeness, and tone.

Outcomes

Adoption, confidence, and a new standard

87.7%
API adoption success rate
The document directly drove an 87.7% API adoption success rate — a clear signal that the persuasion and implementation guidance were working in tandem as designed.
100%
Integral to implementation
Every connectivity provider who used the document strongly agreed it was integral to successful implementation — validating the decision to invest in documentation quality rather than leaving providers to figure it out independently.
↑
More confident builders
Providers reported greater confidence in implementing UI principles — a direct outcome of the UX guidance section written for teams without design resources. And 100% wanted similar documentation for all future integrations.

This project showed that technical documentation is content design. The structural decisions — leading with value, grounding specs in proven examples, writing UX guidance for non-designers — were the same muscles as any product content work. The medium was different; the discipline was the same.

Survey results — providers scored the document 5/5 on having access being very helpful, and the sections they found most valuable were business models overview, payment management overview, and examples of common use cases

Reflection

What I learned

The most interesting challenge in this project was holding two audiences in mind simultaneously — the decision-maker who needed to be convinced, and the engineer who needed to be equipped. Writing for one often conflicts with writing for the other: executives want narrative, engineers want precision. The structure I landed on — layering the business case into the technical overview rather than separating them — was the hardest thing to get right, and the thing that made the document work.

I also learned that "document" is an underrated deliverable in content design. The instinct is always toward UI — microcopy, flows, labels. But sometimes the highest-impact content work happens before anyone opens the product. A well-written technical document that makes the right people want to build is upstream of everything that comes after it.

The 100% feedback asking for this format on future integrations was the best possible signal: not that the document was good, but that it changed the standard for what documentation could be.
